Applied_Promoters.reason: The paper states the complete wheat genomic sequence included ~2.4 kb native promoter and 1.5 kb native terminator. It also shows pathogen-induced expression in sorghum, but does not explicitly characterize the promoter itself as inducible/pathogen-responsive.

Applied_Species.reason: Transgenic experiments were performed in sorghum.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper identifies the host as Sorghum bicolor.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Transformation was done in sorghum cultivar/inbred line Tx430.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Discussion states Lr34 has promise as a transgene for sorghum improvement, low-expression/single-copy lines have less yield penalty, and pathogen-inducible promoters may help avoid reproductive penalties while retaining strong protection.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show Lr34res increases resistance to rust and anthracnose, elevates expression of SbFNSII/SbFNR/SbDFR3 after infection, and is associated with increased 3-deoxyanthocyanidin metabolites including luteolinidin.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are described in Results/Experimental procedures: stable transformation, copy-number and expression analyses, pathogen assays, qRT-PCR, LC-MS metabolite profiling, fungal biomass assays, and symptom scoring.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The study used P. purpurea and C. sublineolum inoculations, rust assays at 20°C/high humidity, anthracnose mesocotyl infection, and sampled 24–96 hpi depending on assay.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Figure 1 and text show highest transcript levels in adult penultimate leaves of high-copy lines; Figure 4a shows induction by 24 hpi and decline by 48 hpi after P. purpurea inoculation.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: In Discussion, the paper states wheat Lr34res typically provides adult plant resistance and that seedling expression is elevated under low temperatures in rust-infected plants.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: The paper reports correlated/enhanced expression of SbFNSII, SbFNR, and SbDFR3, but provides no direct protein interaction data.

Omics_Data.reason: Metabolite profiling by LC-MS is explicitly described, and no public accession is reported.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Lr34res is described as an ABC transporter; transgenic sorghum shows stronger pigmentation and elevated phytoalexin-associated responses; high-expression lines show immunity/no sporulation while the single-copy line shows partial slow-rusting.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results show reduced vigour, smaller panicles, lower panicle weight, reduced peduncle diameter, and reduced 100-seed weight in multicopy/high-expression lines; single-copy line had less penalty.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper repeatedly identifies Lr34res/LR34 as an ABC transporter.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper refers to wheat throughout, but the exact Latin binomial Triticum aestivum does not appear in the provided text.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: As an ABC transporter, membrane localization is plausible, but the paper does not explicitly state subcellular localization in this text.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three summary points are directly consistent with the Results and Discussion.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Metabolite analysis directly measured increased 3-deoxyanthocyanidins, especially luteolinidin, plus methoxyluteolinidin and methoxyapigenidin in Lr34res lines.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Mesocotyl accumulation after C. sublineolum infection is directly shown. Leaf pigmented spots after rust infection are observed, and Discussion links pigments to 3-deoxyanthocyanidins in infected epidermal cells, but metabolite identity at leaf spots was not directly measured here.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: The paper explicitly discusses 3-deoxyanthocyanidins/flavones as flavonoid phytoalexins.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Discussion states these metabolites are defense compounds associated with restricting pathogen spread and resistance.
